reasoning: The 1-year Treasury yield recently spiked from 3.55% to 3.66% due to fading rate cut expectations following a geopolitical conflict ("Iran drama") and higher energy prices. The FOMC meets on March 17-18, 2026, and will release an updated "dot plot". Since the last dot plot in December 2025, inflation expectations have risen, meaning the Fed will likely project fewer rate cuts for 2026, providing a hawkish catalyst. Furthermore, PCE inflation data on March 20 is expected to reflect persistent inflation (3.1%). While a sharp spike often reverts (the purely statistical model suggests a 36% chance of increase), the fundamental shift in monetary policy expectations over the FOMC meeting strongly points toward the 1-year yield maintaining its upward momentum. The main downside risk is a "flight to safety" lowering yields or the Fed trying to soothe markets, but the focus on inflation suggests upward pressure is more likely. Thus, I estimate a 62% probability of an increase by March 22.
